voice: Playful, punchy, and confident — coffee-nerdy enough to name the varietal, friendly enough to hand you a cup and skip the lecture.

headerConfig.bannerText: Fresh batch drops every Wednesday 7am — roasted in Riverside, shipped Canada-wide flat $8.

designTokens.paletteMood: Warm cream paper, a shot of burnt-orange energy, and near-black ink — a 1970s diner menu that learned to pull a killer espresso.

subtitle.value: Third Gear is a two-barrel craft roaster and cafe on Riverside Ave, roasting small single-origin lots and a stubbornly good house blend for people who take coffee seriously and themselves less so.

items.label: From roast day to your door

data.title: More than a bag of beans
data.subtitle: We roast to order, dial in your gear, and keep the good stuff arriving so you never white-knuckle a Monday again.

items.title: Single-origin lots
items.description: Micro-lots from Huila, Yirgacheffe, and Nariño, roasted in 2kg batches and rested three days so the flavor lands where we mapped it.

items.title: The house subscription
items.description: Pick a cadence and a roast level; we roast your bag the morning it ships, so it hits your kitchen days off the roaster, not months.

items.title: Wholesale for cafes
items.description: Consistent espresso lots, weekly delivery across the city, and a rep who actually answers texts when your grinder drifts.

items.title: Home brew dial-in
items.description: Bring your setup or book a bench session; we tune grind, dose, and ratio for your exact gear until the cup sings.

items.title: The Riverside cafe
items.description: Order at the bar, watch the roaster run behind the glass, and grab a still-warm bag on your way out the door.

items.title: Gear & merch
items.description: Hand-picked grinders, drippers, and Third Gear enamel mugs we actually use on the bar every single shift.

data.quote: Great coffee isn't a secret recipe. It's a farmer who nailed the harvest, a green buyer who paid for it, and a roaster who's honest enough not to burn it. We just try not to be the weak link.

data.title: Want it hot and now?
data.subtitle: Drinks and pastries from the Riverside cafe, delivered across the east side. Bags of beans ship from the shop page.

data.title: The regulars have opinions
data.subtitle: And they are, thankfully, mostly positive ones about the coffee.

items.name: Dev
items.quote: The Huila Pink Bourbon showed up on a Thursday still warm from the drum. My French press has never tasted like strawberry jam before and now I can't go back.

items.name: Renata
items.quote: We switched our espresso to Third Gear's Balanced lot and pulls got shockingly consistent. When our grinder drifted on a Saturday, Marisol texted back a dial-in fix in ten minutes.

items.name: Tomas
items.quote: I came in for a flat white, watched them roast a batch behind the glass, and left with a bag of the Yirgacheffe and way too many opinions about grind size.

items.name: Priya
items.quote: Booked a dial-in session convinced my new grinder was broken. Turns out it was me. Twenty minutes later my pour-overs actually taste like the tasting notes on the bag.

data.title: Coffee questions, answered
data.subtitle: The stuff people actually ask us across the bar on a busy Saturday.

items.question: How fresh is the coffee when it ships?
items.answer: We roast to order on our fresh-batch day every Wednesday and ship within 48 hours of the roast. Most subscribers get their bag three to five days off the roaster, which is when it's genuinely at its best.

items.question: Whole bean or ground?
items.answer: Every bag defaults to whole bean because that's how it stays fresh. At checkout you can pick a grind for espresso, pour-over, French press, or drip and we'll dial it on our grinder before it goes out.

items.question: Where do you ship, and what does it cost?
items.answer: We ship Canada-wide for a flat $8, and orders over $60 ship free. Local east-side orders can also be picked up at the Riverside cafe within a day, usually the same afternoon.

items.question: How does the subscription work?
items.answer: Choose your roast level, bag size, and a cadence of every one, two, or four weeks. Your bag is roasted the morning it ships, and you can pause, skip, or swap origins any time from your account with no lock-in.

items.question: What roast levels do you offer?
items.answer: Three: Bright for fruit-forward single origins, Balanced for our everyday sweet-spot cup, and Deep for a chocolatey, low-acid roast. Each bag lists exactly where it falls so there are no surprises.

items.question: Do you sell to cafes and offices?
items.answer: Yes. We run weekly wholesale delivery across the city with consistent espresso lots, loaner equipment options, and staff training. Email wholesale@thirdgearcoffee.ca and we'll set up a tasting.

items.question: Do you have decaf?
items.answer: We do — a sugarcane-process Colombian decaf that keeps most of the sweetness and body without the caffeine. It's on the shop page year-round and available by subscription too.

data.title: Skip the guessing. Get good coffee on repeat.
data.subtitle: Start a subscription today and your first bag ships from Wednesday's fresh batch — roasted, rested, and on its way in 48 hours.

subtitle.value: Single-origin lots, the house blend, sugarcane decaf, and subscriptions — all roasted in small batches on Riverside and shipped within 48 hours.

data.subtitle: Pick a plan, pick a roast level, and never run dry mid-week again. Pause or cancel any time.

data.title: Every bag, no matter the plan
items: Roasted in 2kg batches on our 1970s-rebuilt Probat drum
Roast date stamped on the bag — never a best-before guess
Traceable to farm or co-op, with the price we paid printed inside
Rested three days before it ships so the flavor is dialed
Resealable, degassing valve bags to keep it fresh at home

data.title: Not sure where to start? Grab the sampler.
data.subtitle: Three 100g bags — one from each roast level — so you can find your cup before you commit to a subscription.

title.value: A garage, a rebuilt drum, and a lot of Wednesdays

subtitle.value: How a spare-parts Probat and a stubborn habit turned into the little roaster on Riverside Ave.

data.title: It started with a drum nobody else wanted
data.description: In 2011 Marisol Vega hauled a dead 1970s Probat out of a shuttered bakery, spent a winter rebuilding it in a Riverside garage, and started roasting two kilos at a time for friends who'd pay in cash and honest feedback. The garage overflowed, the cafe opened three years later, and we've kept the same rule ever since: roast small, roast honest, and never ship a bag we wouldn't brew ourselves.
items: Two-barrel roaster, no giant automated lines
Direct relationships with farms in Huila and Nariño
The original rebuilt Probat still runs every batch
data.imageCaption: The rebuilt Probat on its first roast day, 2011.

data.title: We pay for the good stuff, and we tell you what it cost
data.description: Every micro-lot we buy is traceable to a farm or co-op, and we print the green price we paid on the inside of the bag. It's not a marketing flex — it's the whole point. Good coffee starts with a farmer who nailed the harvest and got paid fairly for it, and our job is to not be the weak link between their work and your kitchen.
items: Green price printed inside every bag
Multi-year relationships, not one-off spot buys
Seasonal lots so you drink coffee at its peak

metadata.structureNotes: Home deliberately breaks the generic hero->services->testimonials->cta pattern by leading with a punchy centered hero, then a stats banner and tabBar 'roast board' moment, then product-first commerce (productGrid) before any narrative, pushing servicesGrid, a single featuredQuote, deliveryLinks, testimonials, and faq below the fold to keep the beans front and center.
